module.name: HTML
module.param: Help - Saved Searches and Reports vs Dashboards

module.name: HTML
module.param:
Saved Searches/Reports/Alerts-- What are these?

In Splunk's terminology a "saved search", a "saved report", an "alert" and a "scheduled search" are all really the same thing.

  1. They are all single searches in the SPL language that when run, generate a single set of results.
  2. Depending on the SPL syntax, the result rows they generate might be call legs, or users or distinct periods of time.
  3. They can be set to run on a schedule, be given alerting properties and email aliases to email if conditions are met. (such saved searches are then called "alerts")

Before diving into using these, you should know that on almost every page of the app you can copy the URL right out of your browser and send it to a colleague. If they click it, after they are prompted to login to splunk they'll land exactly where you were and be looking at what you were looking at. The main benefit of using Saved Reports is that if you specifically mark them "shared" with other users then other colleagues will see them in their "Saved Reports" menu.

module.name: HTML
module.param:
Saved Dashboards -- What are these?

What the Cisco CDR App calls a dashboard is more technically a Simple XML Dashboard in the Splunk lexicon.

  1. They are composed of "panels" which are typically charts and tables but can be other things.
  2. Their permissions can be set to grant or restrict read or write access to all users or to users in certain roles.
  3. They can be scheduled to regularly render all of their panels on their own and email out a pdf of themselves.

When would I use them?

  1. They can offer a great way to onboard a new set of users into Splunk - tell them to go to a specific dashboard that you made for them. This way they don't have to learn all of the (sometimes confusing) interfaces in the app.
  2. When scheduled as daily pdf's, they offer also a way to deliver data to a class of users *without* onboarding those users into Splunk necessarily at all.
  3. Rule of thumb - Scheduling 5 or 10 of these can be very useful. We do not recommend scheduling dozens or hundreds of these. Contact Sideview Support if you feel you want/need to do this.
